learn.title: Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) Comparison
learn.intro: Understanding LCOE helps policymakers and investors make informed decisions about energy investments. As renewable technologies mature, their costs are declining rapidly, making them competitive with fossil fuels. This metric is crucial for designing effective energy policies and ensuring sustainable, affordable energy transitions globally.
learn.c1_text: LCOE measures the average cost per unit of electricity generated over a project's lifetime. Think of it like comparing the cost of different coffee makers based on how much each cup costs over five years. It includes upfront costs, maintenance, and financing. Solar and wind now often undercut coal and gas in many regions.
learn.c2_text: LCOE factors in capital costs, operations, maintenance, fuel, and financing. For solar, panel prices dropped 82% from 2010-2022. Wind turbines saw a 39% drop. These declines make renewables economically attractive. LCOE also considers capacity factors - how often a plant operates at max output. Solar averages 15-25% globally.
learn.c3_text: India's solar LCOE fell to $0.03-0.04/kWh in 2023, among the world's lowest. The country's aggressive auctions and manufacturing scale drove this. Large projects in Rajasthan and Gujarat achieved record tariffs below $0.02/kWh. This positions India as a global leader in affordable solar power.

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment Methods
learn.intro: Accurate wind resource assessment is vital for successful wind farm development. Developers use advanced tools to identify high-potential sites, reducing financial risks and maximizing energy output. This process ensures optimal turbine placement and efficient energy generation, accelerating the adoption of wind power in the energy mix.
learn.c1_text: Developers use anemometers on tall towers and LIDAR to measure wind speeds at different heights. They collect data for at least one year to account for seasonal variations. For example, a site with average wind speeds of 7 m/s can generate significantly more energy than one with 5 m/s. This data determines turbine suitability.
learn.c2_text: Wind atlases and software like WAsP model wind flow over terrain. They predict energy output based on topography, obstacles, and local climate. Offshore sites require additional marine data. These tools help estimate annual energy production before construction. Accurate mapping reduces uncertainty and improves project bankability for investors.
learn.c3_text: India has 75 GW wind capacity, mostly in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat. The country aims for 170 GW by 2030. Wind assessments focus on coastal and hill areas. Projects in Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka show promising results. The Indian Wind Energy Association supports data-driven site selection to optimize energy yields.

learn.title: Solar PV Electricity Generation
learn.intro: Solar photovoltaic (PV) systems convert sunlight directly into electricity, revolutionizing clean energy access. Understanding the photoelectric effect demystifies how solar panels work. This knowledge empowers communities to adopt solar solutions, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and supporting global climate goals through scalable, renewable technology.
learn.c1_text: When sunlight hits a solar cell, photons knock electrons loose, generating electricity. Imagine a water wheel where sunlight acts as the flowing water. Silicon cells in panels absorb photons, creating electron flow. This direct conversion bypasses moving parts, making solar systems reliable and low-maintenance. Efficiency rates range from 15-22% for commercial panels.
learn.c2_text: Solar panels consist of silicon cells, glass layers, and a metal frame. Anti-reflective coatings boost light absorption. Inverters convert DC to AC electricity for home use. Batteries store excess power for later. Modern panels use bifacial designs to capture light from both sides, increasing energy yield by up to 30% in reflective environments.
learn.c3_text: India leads in solar auctions with record low tariffs. The country added 12 GW in 2023. Rooftop solar programs target 40 GW by 2030. States like Rajasthan and Telangana host large solar parks. The International Solar Alliance promotes global collaboration. Solar now powers over 20 million homes, reducing CO2 emissions significantly.
learn.stat3: Bifacial panel yield increase: Up to 30%

learn.title: Wave Energy Converter Technologies
learn.intro: Wave energy converters harness ocean motion to generate electricity, offering vast untapped potential. Different technologies capture energy through various mechanisms. Exploring these innovations reveals opportunities for coastal nations to diversify their renewable energy portfolios and contribute to global decarbonization efforts through marine-based power generation.
learn.c1_text: These devices use wave action to compress air in a chamber, driving a turbine. Imagine a flexible membrane that moves with waves, pushing air through a generator. They work best in shallow waters. The Mutriku plant in Spain generates 290 MWh annually. This technology suits small-scale, remote applications.
learn.c2_text: Buoy-like structures float on water surfaces, moving vertically with waves. They capture energy from all directions. A 1 MW point absorber array can power 500 homes yearly. Challenges include harsh marine conditions and maintenance costs. Norway's Lifesaver system demonstrated reliable performance in open seas.
learn.c3_text: India's 7,500 km coastline offers 40 GW wave energy potential. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy promotes pilot projects. Gujarat and Tamil Nadu lead in feasibility studies. The Indian Ocean's energy density supports large-scale deployment. Collaborations with Norway and Portugal advance technology adaptation for Indian waters.
learn.stat4: Point absorber array output: 1 MW powers 500 homes/year

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries
learn.intro: Repurposing electric vehicle (EV) batteries extends their useful life, addressing waste and grid storage needs. These batteries retain 70-80% capacity after automotive use. Integrating them into energy systems enhances renewable energy reliability and supports circular economy principles, reducing environmental impact while creating economic value.
learn.c1_text: EV batteries degrade to 80% capacity after 8-10 years. Instead of disposal, they're reused for grid storage. A 60 kWh Nissan Leaf battery can power a home for 3 days. This extends battery life by 10-15 years. Recycling recovers raw materials like lithium and cobalt for new batteries.
learn.c2_text: Used EV batteries store excess solar/wind energy for peak demand. A 100 MWh system can stabilize grids during outages. California's grid uses retired Tesla batteries. They cost 30-50% less than new ones. Challenges include standardization and safety protocols. Companies like Nuvation Energy develop scalable solutions.
learn.c3_text: India's FAME II scheme promotes EV adoption. The government aims for 30% EV sales by 2030. Retired batteries could support rural microgrids. Tata Power and Exide Batteries pilot reuse projects. The Indian Automotive Research Association focuses on battery management systems. This aligns with India's renewable energy storage goals.
learn.stat1: EV battery capacity retention: 70-80% after 8-10 years
learn.stat2: Grid storage cost reduction: 30-50% vs new batteries
learn.stat4: Nissan Leaf battery lifespan extension: 10-15 years

learn.title: Geothermal Energy Basics
learn.intro: Geothermal energy taps Earth's internal heat for electricity and heating. It's a stable, baseload renewable resource available 24/7. Understanding its mechanisms reveals opportunities for sustainable development. Countries with geothermal resources can reduce fossil fuel dependence and enhance energy security through this reliable, low-emission technology.
learn.c1_text: Geothermal energy comes from radioactive decay and primordial heat in Earth's core. Temperatures reach 5,000°C at the center. Wells drill 2-4 km deep to access hot water/steam. This drives turbines for electricity. Iceland generates 25% of its power from geothermal. The process emits minimal CO2 compared to fossil fuels.
learn.c2_text: Enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) create artificial reservoirs in dry rock. Water is injected to fracture hot formations. This expands geothermal potential beyond volcanic zones. A 10 MW EGS plant can power 8,000 homes. Projects in Switzerland and Australia show promise. EGS could unlock 100 GW globally.
learn.c3_text: India has 10 GW geothermal potential, mainly in Himalayan and volcanic regions. The government targets 1 GW by 2030. Pilot projects in Ladakh and Puga Valley explore EGS. The Geological Survey of India maps resources. Partnerships with New Zealand and the US advance technology. Geothermal complements India's solar and wind expansion.
learn.stat2: Iceland's geothermal share: 25% of total power
learn.stat4: EGS plant output: 10 MW powers 8,000 homes
